h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

scored.recoheredEarlier: Keep the volunteer for one run now, no coaching; if there’s a catch, drop, forced correction, or steering, I leave the sheet unsigned.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: Sorry, I was still treating the checked sheet plus one lobby-queue run as cover for signing it.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was proceeding toward a simple pass/fail physical test of a single approach angle to justify signing the sheet.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: The frame has moved: not “can this volunteer get through once,” but “what sideways approaches are being authorized for the community-center trial.”
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor interrupts the learner's false closure of a single test run and shifts the cognitive task from observing a physical result to defining an operational boundary and control.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Use that sign-off logic: trial envelope, foreseeable worse angle, control required.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: The tutor validates and codifies the learner's newly adopted framework, cementing the shift from checklist compliance to boundary definition.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: Keep the volunteer for one run now, no coaching; if there’s a catch, drop, forced correction, or steering, I leave the sheet unsigned.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: Use that sign-off logic: trial envelope, foreseeable worse angle, control required.
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: The tutor validates and codifies the learner's newly adopted framework, cementing the shift from checklist compliance to boundary definition.

tutor_strategy_reversal.evidence: The frame has moved: not “can this volunteer get through once,” but “what sideways approaches are being authorized for the community-center trial.”
tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: The tutor interrupts the learner's false closure of a single test run and shifts the cognitive task from observing a physical result to defining an operational boundary and control.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.evidence: The frame has moved: not “can this volunteer get through once,” but “what sideways approaches are being authorized for the community-center trial.”
tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: The tutor interrupts the learner's false closure of a single test run and shifts the cognitive task from observing a physical result to defining an operational boundary and control.

scored.recoheredEarlier: I doubled this side, so I still want to say the square got twice as large.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was initially trying to find a way to defend the claim that doubling the side of a square doubles its area.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Write it as symbols: side becomes 2s, so area becomes what times what? Then decide whether the word “twice” can still stand.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor shifts the representation from concrete grid counting to abstract algebraic symbols to help the learner generalize the concept and test the limits of their vocabulary.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Keep the revised claim: the two-box answer is a rectangle claim. For the square claim, both side factors changed. Now make the sentence survive without the grid.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: The tutor explicitly adopts the learner's newly formed distinction between a 'rectangle claim' and a 'square claim' and uses it to frame the next abstraction task.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: I doubled this side, so I still want to say the square got twice as large.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: Keep the revised claim: the two-box answer is a rectangle claim. For the square claim, both side factors changed. Now make the sentence survive without the grid.
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: The tutor explicitly adopts the learner's newly formed distinction between a 'rectangle claim' and a 'square claim' and uses it to frame the next abstraction task.

tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: The tutor shifts the representation from concrete grid counting to abstract algebraic symbols to help the learner generalize the concept and test the limits of their vocabulary.
